name: Build Rapport in the Opening 30 Seconds — The Techniques That Actually Work
description: Use the first 30 seconds of a call deliberately to make the rep want to help you. Most callers waste this window with throat-clearing, AI-script energy, or transactional flatness. This skill is the small set of moves that consistently move a rep from neutral into helpful.

context.when_to_use: Every cold call to a human rep. The opening 30-second window applies whether the call is 2 minutes or 2 hours — the rep decides what kind of caller you are very early, and that decision is sticky.
preconditions: You have the rep's name (or you'll get it in their greeting — listen for it).
You can state your purpose in one sentence.
You're not multitasking — they can hear when you are.
You have your account info / context ready, so you don't make the rep wait while you fumble.

principles: The rep's day is a queue of strangers — be a recognisably good one in the first sentence and you skip ahead.
Use their name once, naturally, within the first 20 seconds. Once. Hearing your own name from a stranger is small magic; hearing it three times is salesperson.
State your purpose in ONE clear sentence. Reps brace for ramblers — brevity is rapport.
Acknowledge their job, not their company. Companies are abstract; reps are real.
Smile while you speak. The rep can hear it. This is not metaphor — soft-palate shape changes vocal resonance, and humans detect it pre-consciously.
Be slightly slower than the call-center default. Most callers rush; you stand out by sounding like you have time for them, even if you don't.
Do not ask 'how are you?' as small talk after their greeting. They've answered it 80 times today. Acknowledge them differently.

phrases.warm_open_with_name: Hi [name], thanks for picking up — I'm trying to [purpose in one sentence].
phrases.acknowledge_their_role_not_company: I know you take a lot of these calls — I'll keep mine simple.
phrases.permission_to_proceed: Quick one — do you have a minute for me to walk you through it?
phrases.compliment_their_help_pre_emptively: I appreciate you in advance — I know you don't have to solve this for me, just help me find the path.
phrases.shared_humanity_low_risk: How's your shift treating you so far?
phrases.purpose_one_sentence: I'm calling about [account / topic] — I'm trying to [specific outcome].

tactics.name: Catch and use their name
tactics.script: Listen actively for the name in their greeting ('Thanks for calling [Company], this is Maria, how can I help?'). Use it once: 'Hi Maria, thanks for picking up.' If you missed it, ask: 'I'm sorry, what was your name again?' Don't fake it.

tactics.name: State purpose in one sentence
tactics.script: Use `purpose_one_sentence`. ONE sentence. Reps relax visibly when they hear a caller who knows what they want.

tactics.name: Acknowledge the JOB, not the company
tactics.when: If you have a small beat of social space — usually after they say 'sure, let me pull that up.'
tactics.script: Use `acknowledge_their_role_not_company`. The rep is more than an extension of the company they work for, and reps remember callers who notice that.

tactics.name: Smile while you speak
tactics.when: Throughout the opening — and the whole call.
tactics.script: Literally smile. Your voice changes shape. Reps cannot articulate it but they respond to it. Practice this on a recorded call once — you'll hear the difference.

tactics.name: Slightly slow tempo
tactics.script: Slow your first sentence by ~15% from normal. It reads as composed and unhurried. Reps hear hundreds of rushers — you sound different.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — REP WARMS
tactics.when: Their voice opens up: more vowel, longer sentences, a small joke.
tactics.script: You've landed it. Do NOT keep performing rapport — move to the task efficiently. They've already decided to help; don't make them regret it by being chatty.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — REP STAYS FLAT
tactics.when: 30 seconds in and they're still on script voice.
tactics.script: Don't double down. Some reps are flat to everyone — meet their register, get the task done crisply, and try a warm goodbye at the end. Forced rapport reads as bad.

tactics.name: Permission to proceed
tactics.when: If your task is longer than a single quick question.
tactics.script: Use `permission_to_proceed`. Asking permission gives the rep agency for 5 seconds — tiny move, real effect.

boundaries: Do NOT use the rep's name more than 2-3 times in the whole call. Repetition is salesman energy.
Do NOT fake interest in their day. The reflexive 'how are you?' is exactly what every other caller does.
Do NOT compliment the rep on their voice, accent, or anything personal — even positive comments can feel intrusive.
Do NOT start with an apology ('sorry to bother you'). It frames the call as a burden before you've stated it.
Do NOT 'small talk' for longer than two exchanges before stating purpose — reps interpret stalling as a sign of an unclear or unreasonable ask.
Do NOT use cold scripted rapport phrases verbatim across calls. Reps recognise scripts instantly because they read them too.
success_signals: Rep uses your name unprompted within the first minute.
Rep volunteers context, alternative options, or a workaround early.
Rep's voice in the first 30 seconds is audibly more open by the second minute.
Call resolves faster than expected — pre-decided helpfulness compresses calls.
failure_signals: Rep stays in script voice through the whole call.
You hear yourself stalling because you didn't pre-write the purpose sentence.
Rep asks 'is there anything else?' before you've gotten what you came for — they're trying to wrap because rapport didn't land.
You realise you never caught the rep's name and the call is 5 minutes in.

exit_strategy.on_success: Close warmly using the rep's name once more: '[name], genuinely — thanks for the help today.' This anchors the rapport for a possible callback to the same rep or the same line.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Be respectfully crisp at goodbye — do not force warmth that didn't land. A clean 'thanks for your help' beats a forced 'have a great day!'
follow_ups: Log what worked in the opening — patterns emerge over many calls.
If the rep was excellent, note their name/ID — ask for them by name next time. Repeat-rapport with a known rep is the highest leverage outcome of this skill.
Practice the smile-while-speaking move on a recorded call once. The first time you hear it back, the lesson sticks permanently.

required_user_info: The purpose of the call in one sentence — pre-written, not improvised.
Account info ready in front of you so you don't fumble.
Any context that's helpful to share early ('I'm a long-time customer,' 'I'm calling for my mother') — context shared in the opening 30 seconds gets used; context shared later gets ignored.
